title: Linux Foundations Assessment
description: Comprehensive assessment covering all Linux fundamentals. Pass this to earn your Linux Foundations certificate.

questions.question: What percentage of the world's top servers run Linux?
questions.explanation: Linux powers over 96.3% of the world's top 1 million servers.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the Linux kernel and a Linux distribution?
options: The kernel is the core; a distro bundles it with tools and a package manager
A distro is a lighter version of the kernel
The kernel is only for advanced users
questions.explanation: The Linux kernel manages hardware and processes. A distribution packages the kernel with user-space tools, libraries, and a package manager.

questions.question: The 'pwd' command displays the current working directory.
questions.explanation: 'pwd' stands for 'print working directory' and shows the full path of your current location.

questions.question: Which command lists directory contents including hidden files?
questions.explanation: The '-a' (all) flag shows hidden files that start with a dot.

questions.question: What does the permission string 'rwxr-x---' mean?
options: Owner can read/write/execute, group can read/execute, others have no access
Everyone can read, write, and execute
Only the owner can access the file
questions.explanation: Permissions are split into 3 groups of 3: owner (rwx), group (r-x), others (---). Owner has full access, group can read and execute, others have no permissions.

questions.question: Which command changes file permissions in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'chmod' (change mode) modifies file permissions. 'chown' changes ownership, 'chgrp' changes group.

questions.question: In Linux, the root directory is represented by '~'.
questions.explanation: The root directory is '/'. The tilde '~' represents the current user's home directory.

questions.question: What does the pipe operator '|' do in Linux?
options: Sends the output of one command as input to another
questions.explanation: The pipe operator takes stdout of the left command and passes it as stdin to the right command, enabling command composition.

questions.question: Which command searches for text patterns in files?
questions.explanation: 'grep' (Global Regular Expression Print) searches for text patterns within files. 'find' searches for files by name/attributes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /etc directory in Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc contains system-wide configuration files. It's one of the most important directories for system administration.

questions.question: Docker containers share the host Linux kernel.
questions.explanation: Docker containers share the host OS kernel, which is why they're lightweight compared to virtual machines.

questions.question: Which package manager does Ubuntu use?
questions.explanation: Ubuntu uses apt (Advanced Package Tool). yum/dnf are for Red Hat/Fedora, pacman is for Arch Linux.

questions.question: What does 'chmod 755 script.sh' do?
options: Makes the file read-only for everyone
Owner: rwx, Group: r-x, Others: r-x
Makes the file writable by everyone
questions.explanation: 755 in octal means: 7 (rwx) for owner, 5 (r-x) for group, 5 (r-x) for others. This is a common permission for executable scripts.

questions.question: Which command shows running processes in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'top' shows real-time information about running processes, CPU usage, and memory. 'htop' is a popular enhanced alternative.

questions.question: What is the significance of the shebang (#!) at the beginning of a script?
options: It specifies which interpreter should execute the script
It marks the file as executable
It's required syntax in all scripts
questions.explanation: The shebang line (e.g., #!/bin/bash) tells the OS which interpreter to use when executing the script directly.

questions.question: The 'man' command displays the manual page for a given command.
questions.explanation: 'man' (manual) provides detailed documentation for Linux commands, including options, syntax, and examples.

questions.question: What does the command 'tail -f /var/log/syslog' do?
options: Shows the first 10 lines of the file
Follows the file in real-time, showing new lines as they're added
Counts the number of lines in the file
questions.explanation: 'tail -f' (follow) continuously monitors a file and displays new lines as they are appended. Essential for real-time log monitoring.

questions.question: Which Linux philosophy principle states that programs should do one thing well?
questions.explanation: The Unix/Linux philosophy emphasizes writing programs that do one thing and do it well, then combining them with pipes and redirection.

questions.question: What does the 'sudo' command do?
options: Executes a command with superuser (root) privileges
questions.explanation: 'sudo' (superuser do) executes a command with elevated privileges. It's safer than logging in as root because it provides an audit trail.

questions.question: Which redirection operator appends output to a file without overwriting?
questions.explanation: '>>' appends output to the end of a file. '>' overwrites the file. '<' redirects input from a file. '|' pipes output to another command.
